coredata.dc:title: Ultrafast microwave-assisted multicomponent tandem polymerization for rapid fabrication of AIE-active fluorescent polymeric nanoparticles and their potential utilization for biological imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Ruming
Huang, Long
Liu, Meiying
Deng, Fengjie
Huang, Hongye
Tian, Jianwen
Wen, Yuanqing
Cao, Qian-yong
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: The fabrication and biomedical applications of fluorescent polymeric nanoparticles (FPNs) with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) feature has attracted the most intensive research interest since the first discovery of AIE phenomenon by Tang' group. Although great attention has been devoted to preparation of AIE-active FPNs, an efficient, facile and versatile strategy is still highly desirable to advance their biomedical applications. In this work, a one-pot microwave-assisted multicomponent tandem polymerization was proposed to fabricate AIE-active FPNs based on a microwave-assisted Kabachnik–Fields (KF) reaction, which involves the conjugation of aldehyde group containing polyethylene glycol (CHO-PEG-CHO) and amino-group terminating AIE dye (H2N-PhE-NH2) using diethyl phosphate as the lock. The KF reaction can occur under rather facile and mild experimental conditions (e.g. absent of catalyst and solvents, air atmosphere) with the assistance of microwave irradiation in 5min. The resultant (PEG-DP-PhE) copolymers would self-assemble into FPNs that showed high water dispersibility and enhanced fluorescence intensity. The desirable cytocompatibility and cell uptake efficiency of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs endow their great potential for biomedical applications. Considering the convenience and effectiveness, the method should be promising for fabrication of many AIE-active functional materials with great application potential.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregation-induced emission
Multicomponent reaction
Microwave irradiation
Biological imaging
Fluorescent polymeric nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 1H NMR spectra of (A) CHO-PEG-CHO and (B) PEG-DP-PhE measured in D2O at room temperature.
FT-IR spectra of CHO-PEG-CHO and PEG-DP-PhE copolymers.

(A) TEM image of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs. (B) UV–Vis spectrum of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs. (Inset) Photographs of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs in water solution. (C) The fluorescent spectra of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs. The excitation wavelength of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs is 450nm, while the emission wavelength was located at 572nm. (D) The fluorescent spectra of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs in aqueous solution were used to determine their photostability under exposing to the UV lamp for 1h.

Viability of L929 cells after incubation with PEG-DP-PhE FPNs at different concentrations of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs (0–120μgmL−1) for 12 and 24h. The cell evaluation experiment suggested that FPNs showed negative toxicity to cells.
CLSM images of L929 cells incubated with 40μgmL−1 of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs. (A) excited with 458nm, (B) bright field, (C) merged imaged of (A) and (B). Scale bar=50μm.
The route for preparation of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs through a MCP based on the microwave-assisted KF reaction.

head.ce:title: Ultrafast microwave-assisted multicomponent tandem polymerization for rapid fabrication of AIE-active fluorescent polymeric nanoparticles and their potential utilization for biological imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang 330031, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Center for Nanotechnology, Chung-Yuan Christian University, Chung-Li 32023, Taiwan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Y. Wei, Department of Chemistry, The Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fabrication and biomedical applications of fluorescent polymeric nanoparticles (FPNs) with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) feature has attracted the most intensive research interest since the first discovery of AIE phenomenon by Tang' group. Although great attention has been devoted to preparation of AIE-active FPNs, an efficient, facile and versatile strategy is still highly desirable to advance their biomedical applications. In this work, a one-pot microwave-assisted multicomponent tandem polymerization was proposed to fabricate AIE-active FPNs based on a microwave-assisted Kabachnik–Fields (KF) reaction, which involves the conjugation of aldehyde group containing polyethylene glycol (CHO-PEG-CHO) and amino-group terminating AIE dye (H2N-PhE-NH2) using diethyl phosphate as the lock. The KF reaction can occur under rather facile and mild experimental conditions (e.g. absent of catalyst and solvents, air atmosphere) with the assistance of microwave irradiation in 5min. The resultant (PEG-DP-PhE) copolymers would self-assemble into FPNs that showed high water dispersibility and enhanced fluorescence intensity. The desirable cytocompatibility and cell uptake efficiency of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs endow their great potential for biomedical applications. Considering the convenience and effectiveness, the method should be promising for fabrication of many AIE-active functional materials with great application potential.

A facile multicomponent tandem polymerization strategy has been developed for preparation of fluorescent polymeric nanoparticles with aggregation-induced emission feature based on the microwave-assisted multicomponent reaction.
Image 2

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence imaging is an emerging optical imaging technology in recent years [1,2]. It has been extensively explored for biological sensors and theranostics because of its high sensitivity, high spatial resolution and suitable for multi parameters measurement [3,4]. The applications of fluorescent nanoparticles for biological imaging have also made significant progress over the past few decades [5]. However, many of them have their own disadvantages that limit their further applications in the biomedical fields. For example, fluorescent proteins could be regarded as the water soluble and biocompatible fluorescent macromolecules at nanoscale range, while they are obtained through cumbersome transfection and purification processes and are therefore rather expensive [6]. On the other hand, fluorescent inorganic materials such as semiconductor quantum dots, metal clusters and carbon quantum dots are poor degradable and toxicity to living organisms owing to their heavy metal compositions and long term accumulation [7–12]. More importantly, the fluorescent properties of most fluorescent inorganic materials are difficult to be fine tuned through chemical structure design [13,14]. The fluorescent polymeric nanoparticles (FPNs) that composited with hydrophobic segments (organic dyes) and other hydrophilic components seem to be the most promising candidates as the fluorescent probes. However, the fluorescence intensity of these FPNs based on conventional organic dyes will be largely decreased owing to the notorious aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) effect from π-π stacking [15]. Therefore, the searching for novel organic dyes that could overcome the ACQ effect of conventional organic dyes is crucial for the fabrication of FPNs with desirable optical properties [16,17].

Since the first report of aggregation-induced emission (AIE) phenomenon in 2001 by Tang et al., this abnormal fluorescence phenomenon has rapidly attracted increasing research interest and has been extensively explored for various applications by Tang et al. [18–20]. Especially, the AIE-active functional materials have been widely for biomedical applications through the incorporation of AIE-active molecules into amphiphilic copolymers, which could self-assemble into AIE-active FPNs with desirable optical properties [21]. So far, there have been many reports on the construction of AIE-active FPNs have been developed by Wei et al. [22–24]. For example, we have previously demonstrated that the AIE-active FPNs could be facilely obtained through the non-covalent encapsulation using the commercially available surfactant F127 [25]. However, these methods are mainly relied on the hydrophobic interaction between AIE-active dyes and hydrophobic segments of amphiphilies. These AIE-active FPNs may not be stable in diluted solution when the concentration is lower than the critical micelle concentrations (CMC) of these amphiphilies. Many other constructed by covalent methods such as Schiff base reaction, free radical polymerization, controlled living radical polymerization as well as ring-opening reaction have also been developed by Zhang and Wei et al. [26–31]. However, these methods generally need tedious experimental procedures, harsh reaction conditions and long reaction time.

The multicomponent reactions (MCRs) were favored by organic synthetic chemists due to their simple operation of one-pot method, atom economy, mild conditions and low reaction costs and so on [32,33]. In recent years, the concept of MCRs has been introduced into the field of polymer chemistry. Therefore, MCRs came into being and have been extensively adopted in the synthesis of multifunctional polymers [34]. MCRs refer to introduce polymerizable elements in the MCRs, and functional polymers with clear structure and specific functional structure were facilely synthesized by the strategy of combining the functional monomers with controlled polymerization. Compared with the traditional controlled polymerization methods, such as atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) [35] and reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer radical polymerization (RAFT) [36], MCRs not only do not need the participation of substances that may be harmful to the organism, such as catalysts and initiators, but also do not need the anaerobic conditions to obtain the final products with high efficiency. Therefore, using MCR strategy simplifies the experimental operation, reduces the cost. Meanwhile, the reaction conditions are more moderate, and the synthesis path is more green and economical. The Kabachnik–Fields (KF) multicomponent polymerization has been developed for the synthesis of high molecular weight polymers recently [37,38]. Take advantage of those merits mentioned above, we have proposed a novel microwave-assisted tandem polymerization method based on a Kabachnik–Fields (KF) reaction, which could greatly reduce the reaction time with the assistance of microwave irradiation [39]. On the other hand, the one-pot operation and solvent-free reaction conditions make this strategy more prominent.

In this contribution, a solvent-free and catalyst-free microwave-assisted multicomponent tandem polymerization (MCP) was developed to construct AIE-active FPNs. An AIE-active dye with two amino groups (Scheme S1) that named as H2N-PhE-NH2 was conjugated with the dialdehyde-terminated polyethylene glycol (named as CHO-PEG-CHO) through the KF reaction (Scheme 1 ). Due to the hydrophobicity of H2N-PhE-NH2 and hydrophilicity of PEG, the final conjugations (PEG-DP-PhE) are expected to be amphiphilic. The successful fabrication of these amphiphilic copolymers through the KF reaction based tandem polymerization was confirmed through a number of characterization techniques. The water dispersibility, optical properties as well as compatibility were also investigated to evaluate their potential biomedical applications. We demonstrated that the amphiphilic copolymers could be facilely obtained through the microwave-assisted MCP and the resultant fluorescent assemblies displayed good physicochemical properties and desirable biological imaging performance. Moreover, the microwave-assisted MCP should be a very promising method for fabrication of AIE-active FPNs for their high efficiency, short reaction time, mild experimental reaction conditions and substrate adaptability.

N,N′-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), 4-formylbenzoic acid (CBA), polyethylene glycol (PEG, Mw: 1000Da), 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP), diethyl phosphite and tetrahydrofuran (THF) offered from Aladdin (Shanghai, China) were all used as received. All other commercially available reagents were used without purification. The AIE dye NH2-PhE-NH2 was synthesized and characterized in our previous work [40]. The 1H and 31P NMR spectra were carried out on a Bruker Avance-400 spectrometer [D2O and d6-DMSO as the solvents and tetramethylsilane (TMS) as a reference]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a Hitachi 7650B microscope operated at 80kV. The FT-IR spectra were obtained in a reflection mode on a Nicolet5700 (Thermo Nicolet corporation) using KBr pellets. The UV–visible absorption spectra of nanoparticles in water solution were obtained on a Perkin Elmer LAMBDA 35 UV/Vis system using quartz cuvettes of 1cm path length. The fluorescence spectra were measured by using a Fluorescence spectrophotometer (FSP, model: C11367-11) with a slit width of 3nm for both excitation and emission. The hydrodynamic size distribution of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs in phosphate buffer saline was also investigated by dynamic laser scattering (DLS), which was conducted on a zetaPlus particle size analyzer (ZetaPlus, Brookhaven Instruments, Holtsville, NY). Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) analyses of polymers were performed using N,N-dimethyl formamide (DMF) containing 50mM LiBr as the eluant. The GPC system was a Shimadzu LC-20AD pump system consisting of an auto injector, a MZ Gel SDplus 10.0μm guard column (50×8.0mm, 102 Å) followed by a MZ-Gel SDplus 5.0μm bead-size column (50–106 Å, linear) and a Shimadzu RID-10A refractive index detector.

The hydrophilic polymer (named as CHO-PEG-CHO) was synthesized through simple esterification reaction (Scheme S2). In brief, PEG (1g, 1mmol), CBA (330mg, 2.2mmol), DCC (1g, 5mmol) and DMAP (61mg, 0.5mmol) were dissolved in 20mL of THF in a round-bottomed flask consisting of a magnetic stir bar. The reaction mixture was heated to 40°C at nitrogen atmosphere for 24h. Afterward, the reaction was stopped by pouring the reaction solution into 100mL of diethyl ether to obtain white precipitates. The precipitates were washed with diethyl ether and dried under vacuum to a constant weight.

The PEG-DP-PhE copolymers were fabricated via a rapid and facile microwave-assisted MCR method. The dye NH2-PhE-NH2 (71mg, 1mmol), CHO-PEG-CHO (126mg, 1mmol) and DP (414mg, 3mmol) were added into a round-bottomed flask. The reaction mixture was irradiated in a multimode microwave reactor (MCR-3, China) at 100°C for only 5min with drastically string. Upon cooling, the mixture solution was put in the dialysis bag (MWCO 3500) and placed into the fresh water for 48h, followed by evaporating to obtain the raw product. The product was then purified by dissolving in THF and precipitated in cool diethyl ether for several times. Finally, the obtained PEG-DP-PhE copolymers were dried at vacuum for further characterization.

The biocompatibility of materials is a very important property for their application in the biomedical fields. In this work, the biocompatibility of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs was evaluated through determination of their cell viability towards L929 cells. The cell viability values of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs were determined using cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay based on our previous reports [41–44]. In brief, L929 cells were cultured into 96-well microplates at a density of 5×104 cellsmL−1 in 160μL of respective media containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). After the cell attachment for 24h, the PEG-DP-PhE FPNs with different concentrations (10–120μgmL−1) were incubated with cells for 12 and 24h. After removal of the luminescent particles outsides cells with PBS, 10μL of CCK-8 solution and 100μL of Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) cell culture medium were added to each well and incubated for another 3h. Afterward, plates were analyzed using a microplate reader (VictorШ, Perkin-Elmer). Measurements of formazan dye absorbance were carried out at 450nm, with the reference wavelength at 620nm. The values were proportional to the number of live cells. The percent reduction of CCK-8 dye was compared to controls, which represented 100% CCK-8 reduction. Three replicate wells were used per microplate, and the experiment was operated for three times. Cell survival was expressed as absorbance relative to that of untreated controls. Results are presented as mean±standard deviation (SD).

To evaluate the biological imaging applications of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs, the cell uptake behavior of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs was examined by a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) Zeiss 710 3-channel (Zeiss, Germany) using L929 cells. The excitation wavelength was set as 458nm. L929 cells were set in a glass bottom dish with a density of 1×105 cells per dish. On the day of treatment, PEG-DP-PhE FPNs with a suitable concentration of 40μgmL−1 was incubated with cells for 3h at 37°C. Afterward, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove the PEG-DP-PhE FPNsand then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10min at room temperature.

The amphipathic copolymers PEG-DP-PhE were synthesized through a two-step process. First, the dialdehyde-terminated PEG (CHO-PEG-CHO) was obtained by esterification reaction between hydroxyl terminated polyethylene glycol and 4-formylbenzoic acid. H2N-PhE-NH2, CHO-PEG-CHO and diethyl phosphite were mixed in the reaction flask after microwave irradiation for 5min, and the PEG-DP-PhE copolymers were finally synthesized by a microwave-assisted MCP. The chemical structure of CHO-PEG-CHO, PEG-DP-PhE can be confirmed by the 1H NMR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1 , the peaks with chemical shift at 8.23–7.69ppm can be observed in the 1H NMR of CHO-PEG-CHO and PEG-DP-PhE. This indicated that the aromatic rings were contained in the two samples. Meanwhile, a single peak with the chemical shift at 9.91ppm attributed to the signal of CHO was also found in the spectrum of CHO-PEG-CHO, indicating the successful synthesis of CHO-PEG-CHO. After linked with NH2-PhE-NH2, a series of peaks at 8.21–7.21ppm can be found and a new peak is located at 1.14ppm. It should be ascribed to the introduction of NH2-PhE-NH2. In addition, the signals at 4.0–3.0ppm that referred to the methyl and methylene of PEG were also observed in the 1H NMR spectrum of PEG-DP-PhE. Moreover, a peak at 24.0ppm was clearly found in 31P NMR spectrum of PEG-DP-PhE (Fig. S2). The above results implied that NH2-PhE-NH2 and CHO-PEG-CHO have successfully conjugated together using DP as the lock.

Successful fabrication of PEG-DP-PhE was further confirmed by FT-IR spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 , a characteristic peaks at 2866cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration band of CH2 in CHO-PEG-CHO, respectively. Moreover, a sharp peak at 1717cm−1 was found in the spectrum of CHO-PEG-CHO, indicating that the CO group was existed in CHO-PEG-CHO, while the obvious peak at 1108cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of CO. Compared with the spectrum of CHO-PEG-CHO, the stretching vibration bond of CO group is disappeared, while two new peaks are appeared at 1245cm−1 (PO) and 2924cm−1 (CH3), demonstrating that CHO-PEG-CHO, DP and NH2-PhE-NH2 were conjugated together through the microwave-assisted MCP. On the other hand, a peak at 1630cm−1 was observed in the PEG-DP-PhE FPNs, which are attributed to the stretching vibration of aromatic rings stemmed from NH2-PhE-NH2. On the other hand, Mn of PEG-DP-PhE was also characterized by GPC is 12,623gmol−1 with relatively narrow PDI of 1.16 (Fig. S6). All these results confirmed the successful fabrication of PEG-DP-PhE through the microwave-assisted MCP.

Polyethylene glycol (PEG) is a kind of nonionic water-soluble polymer. Due to its excellent properties such as water solubility, physiological inert and low toxicity, there have been many reports on the functionalization of different materials using PEG [45,46]. In this work, the amphiphilic copolymers PEG-DP-PhE FPNs were synthesized by a microwave-assisted MCP for conjugation of hydrophilic PEG and hydrophobic H2N-PhE-NH2. As is known to all, amphiphilic molecules can be self-assembled in aqueous solution to form molecular aggregates with double layer structures and regular morphology. So the TEM was used to observe the morphology and size of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs, which served to further confirm their formation. As shown in Fig. 3A, it showed that a number of regular spherical particles with size about 100nm were clearly observed. The TEM image directly evidences the formation of self-assemblies. The successful formation of nanostructure indicates we have successfully obtained amphiphilic copolymers (PEG-DP-PhE). In which, the hydrophobic PhE was accumulated in the core of FPNs and resulted in the enhanced fluorescence intensity owing to the AIE feature of phenothiazine derivatives. While the hydrophilic PEG was covered on the hydrophobic core and expanded into water. This could render the final PEG-DP-PhE FPNs great water dispersibility. Moreover, the hydrodynamic size distribution of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs in PBS was also determined by DLS. We found that the size distribution of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs in PBS 291±138.6nm with polydispersibility index of 0.28 (Fig. S3). As compared with the TEM results, the size distribution of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs based on DLS is relatively big. It is mainly due to the shrinkage of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs during the preparation of TEM sample. Taken together, we could conclude that the PEG-DP-PhE copolymers will self-assemble into nanoscale aggregates based on TEM and DLS results.

Because of the AIE dyes was aggregated in the core of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs, and the hydrophilic PEG shells were wrapped outside the core. It is expected that the PEG-DP-PhE FPNs had both significant fluorescence properties and good water solubility. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum was used to examine the optical property of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs. As shown in Fig. 3B, the maximum absorption peak located at around 244nm can be attributed to the long π→π* conjugate structure of PhE dye encapsulated in the PEG-DP-PhE FPNs, while the absorption peak at 438nm can be ascribed to the existence of phenothiazine. Therefore, we can deduce that PhE has been incorporated into FPNs based on the UV–Vis spectrum. On the other hand, a transparent and uniform orange-yellow solution can be seen from the water suspension of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs (the inset of Fig. 3B). Meanwhile, no particles were observed to be suspended in the solution. In addition, the text in the background can be intuitively observed through the solution, which potently illustrated that, the PEG-DP-PhE FPNs with satisfactory water solubility. Furthermore, the luminescent properties of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs were further evaluated by fluorescent spectra and the results were shown in Fig. 3C. From which it can be seen that the maximum emission wavelength of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs was located at 572nm and the optimal fluorescent excitation wavelength was at 450nm. For biomedical applications, the good photostability is one of the most important factors. As shown in Fig. 3D, compared to the fluorescence intensity before UV irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs was not substantially weakened after 1h of exposure to UV lamp at 365nm. Moreover, it can be directly seen from the inset of Fig. 3D that the bright and strong orange-yellow fluorescence when the aqueous solution of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs irradiated by UV lamp at 365nm. The results demonstrated the feasibility of using the PEGylation strategy to construct FPNs with desirable optical properties by microwave-assisted MCP approach. PEG-DP-PhE FPNs not only had regular spherical morphology, but also owned good fluorescence properties and excellent photostability, which were essential factors for their practical applications as biological materials. It is well known that the phenothiazine based molecules are of AIE feature. Therefore, the AIE property of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs was also examined. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs was rapidly decreased when the fraction of water was increased from 0% to 40%, while the fluorescence intensity of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs was slowly increased when the water fraction was further increased from 40% to 90%. The results clearly suggested that PEG-DP-PhE FPNs are antiACQ. According to the literatures, this phenomenon should belong to an atypical AIE phenomenon due to the twisted intramolecular charge transfer (TICT) and restriction of intramolecular rotations (RIR) of AIE molecular [47–49]. Thus, PEG-DP-PhE FPNs should possess AIE feature. This is very important for the fabrication of FPNs with desirable optical properties. Fig. S5 showed that PEG-DP-PhE FPNs had very low CMC value, which was 0.012mgmL−1. The low CMC value demonstrated that PEG-DP-PhE FPNs can stable exist in highly dilute solution.

Biocompatibility is the main subject throughout the research of biomaterials, and good biocompatibility is an indispensable factor of biomaterials. Due to the surface polymerization of non-toxic and water-soluble PEG on FPNs, it was anticipated that PEG-DP-PhE FPNs should possess excellent biocompatibility. In order to evaluate the biocompatibility of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs, the CCK-8 assay was used to detect cell proliferation and cell viability. The L929 cells were incubated with different concentration of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs ranging 20 to 120μg mL for 12 and 24h, respectively. From Fig. 4 , it can be found out that there was no significant decrease in cell viability values. It is worth to note that even when the concentration of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs was as high as 120μgmL−1, the cell viability values was still greater than 99% after they incubated with PEG-DP-PhE FPNs for 24h. This result confirmed that PEG-DP-PhE FPNs owned favorable biocompatibility, which further indicated that PEG-DP-PhE FPNs have great potential for biomedical applications.

Based on a variety of excellent features of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs confirmed by the above experiments, such as regular spherical morphology, excellent water dispersibility, strong fluorescence, excitation-dependent emission property and good photostability, PEG-DP-PhE FPNs were expected to be a kind of ideal biomaterials for biological imaging. Therefore, the cellular uptake behavior in vitro and the cell imaging applications of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs were further investigated by CLSM observation. As shown in Fig. 5A, after L929 cells were incubated with 40μgmL−1 of PEG-DP-PhE FPNs, strong orange-yellow fluorescence signals could be observed when the cell plates were excited under laser of 458nm. Furthermore, it can also be observed that there are many regions with weak fluorescence or no fluorescence, which should be the locations of cell nuclei. The reason for this phenomenon may be that the size of the PEG-DP-PhE FPNs was larger than that of the nuclear pore. Therefore the particles cannot enter cell nuclei directly. Fig. 5B shows the bright field image in the same region. It can be seen that the cells were well spread on the cell plates with normal morphology. This also implied that PEG-DP-PhE FPNs possess good biocompatibility. Moreover, we also found that the cell locations are just covered by the areas of fluorescence signals (Fig. 5C). This clearly indicated that the PEG-DP-PhE FPNs are indeed internalized by cells. On the other hand, we speculated that the PEG-DP-PhE FPNs were entered into cells mainly by endocytosis and were substantially distributed in the cytoplasm. All of these results indicated that PEG-DP-PhE FPNs can be internalized by cells and are of great potential for biological imaging applications. It is well known that the nanoparticles can be accumulated in tumor sites owing to the enhanced permeability and retention effect. Therefore, we could expect that the PEG-DP-PhE FPNs are of great potential for passive targeting capability.

In conclusion, we have proposed a novel and effective method through the combination of microwave irradiation and MCP to construct AIE-active FPNs. The microwave-assisted MCP can occur under rather mild experimental conditions (catalyst-free, solvent free, air atmosphere and mild reaction temperature) within short reaction term (5min). Since the hydrophobic H2N-PhE-NH2 was encapsulated in the interior of the FPNs and covered by the hydrophilic PEG, PEG-DP-PhE FPNs exhibited high water dispersibility and AIE feature. Furthermore, the PEG-DP-PhE FPNs also displayed many favorable properties, including small size and regular spherical morphology, intense fluorescence, good photostability, excellent biocompatibility and cell stain performance. These features make PEG-DP-PhE FPNs very promising in biomedical fields. Taking into account simplicity, environmental friendliness, as well as scalability of this construction method, the microwave-assisted MCR described in this work can also be used for further functionalization of other FPNs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51363016, 21474057, 21564006, 21561022, 21644014), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province in China (Nos. 20161BAB203072, 20161BAB213066).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2017.11.010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mater Sci Eng C Mater Biol Appl
Mater Sci Eng C Mater Biol Appl
